en.extract:
The Acre tody-tyrant (Hemitriccus cohnhafti) is a species of bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in Bolivia, Brazil, and Peru.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Acre tody-tyrant was first described in 2013 and taxonomic systems quickly began recognizing the new species. The describing authors chose the specific epithet cohnhafti to honor their colleague Mario Cohn-Haft "in recognition of his numerous and ongoing contributions to our understanding of the marvelous avifauna of Amazonian Brazil". The species' English name refers to the Brazilian state where it was discovered.
The Acre tody-tyrant is monotypic.


Description
The Acre tody-tyrant is about 11 cm (4.3 in) long; one male weighed 9 g (0.32 oz). The sexes apparently have the same plumage. Adults have a greenish olive head and nape with darker streaks on the crown. They have tawny lores and a tawny wash on an otherwise greenish olive face. Their back and rump are greenish olive. Their wings are blackish with yellow outer edges and whitish inner edges on the flight feathers. Their wing coverts are black with yellowish tawny edges that show as two wing bars. Their tail feathers are blackish with dark green inner vanes. Their throat and breast are olive-green with creamy yellowish streaks, their upper belly and flanks are unstreaked olive-green, and their lower belly and undertail coverts are sulphur-yellow. They have a cream to brownish hazel iris and gray legs and feet. Their bill is flattish with a wide base and large oval nostrils; it is black with a creamy base to the mandible.


Distribution and habitat
The Acre tody-tyrant was initially known only from the type locality in southeastern Acre in western Brazil. The locality is very close to both northwestern Bolivia and southeastern Peru and the original authors speculated that the species also occurred in those two countries. It has since been confirmed in both Bolivia and Peru.
The Acre tody-tyrant primarily inhabits secondary forest on nutrient-poor sandy soils. These areas typically have a canopy height of under about 12 m (40 ft) and occasionally as low as 5 m (15 ft). In Brazil at least they tend to have dead or dying stands of Guadua bamboo. It is not known to inhabit várzea or terra firme forest. In elevation it is known only below about 300 m (1,000 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Acre tody-tyrant is believed to be a year-round resident.


Feeding
The Acre tody-tyrant feeds on arthropods. It feeds mostly in thick undergrowth within about 1 to 4 m (3 to 13 ft) above the ground, using short upward sallies from a perch to glean prey from the underside of vegetation. It is not known to join mixed-species feeding flocks.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the Acre tody-tyrant's breeding biology.


Vocalization
The Acre tody-tyrant's song is a trill of "tick" notes similar to songs of other Hemitriccus tody-tyrants though shorter and lower pitched. The trill usually does not have introductory notes.  It also makes "single-note skep or keek calls...or lower frequency kup calls" and sometimes combines them.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Acre tody-tyrant as Near Threatened. Its population size is not known "but given the paucity of records and small range it is unlikely to be very large", and it is believed to be decreasing though at an unknown rate. "The species' tolerance of successional and shrubby habitats suggests that it may be able to cope with habitat degradation to some degree. There is however extensive clear-cutting ongoing in large parts of the range, particularly for the creation of cattle pastures." 


References

The '''Acre tody-tyrant''' ('''''Hemitriccus cohnhafti''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers. It is found in [[Bolivia]], [[Brazil]], and [[Peru]].<ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=2024-08-19}}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==
The Acre tody-tyrant was [[Species description|first described]] in 2013 and taxonomic systems quickly began recognizing the new species.<ref name="Zimmeretal">{{cite encyclopedia |last1=Zimmer |first1=Kevin J. |last2=Whittaker |first2=Andrew |last3=Haisler Sardelli |first3=Carla |last4=Guilherme |first4=Edson |last5=Aleixo |first5=Alexandre |display-authors=1 |date=June 2013 |location=Barcelona |editor-last1=del Hoyo |editor-first1=Josep |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-last4=Christie |editor-first4=David A. |display-editors=1 |title=A new species of ''Hemitriccus tody-tyrant'' from the state of Acre, Brazil |encyclopedia=Handbook of the Birds of the World |language=en |volume=Special Volume: New Species and Global Index |edition= |publisher=Lynx Edicions |pages=292–296 |isbn=978-84-96553-88-0 |url=https://archive.org/details/handbookofbirdso0001unse_w3n0/page/292/ |access-date=2025-03-28}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCRecentChanges.htm |title=Recognize newly described ''Hemitriccus cohnhafti'' (Proposal 598; 22 Feb 2014) |last1=Miranda |first1=Leonardo S. |last2=Aleixo |first2=Alexandre |date=2014-02-22 |website=Recent Changes |publisher=South American Classification Committee |access-date=2025-01-21}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/updates/update-diary/ |title=Accept Acre Tody-Tyrant |author=<!-- not stated --> |date=2014-02-23 |website=IOC World Bird List Diary |publisher=International Ornithologists' Union |access-date=2025-01-21}}</ref><ref name="HBWChecklistV9">{{cite web |author1=Handbook of the Birds of the World |author2=BirdLife International |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International Digital Checklist of the Birds of the World |url=https://cdn.sanity.io/files/6ibvd6r4/production/47fd3b7af9e34cd85c34f2e04495f8b23ba08a27.pdf/Handbook%20of%20the%20Birds%20of%20the%20World%20and%20BirdLife%20International%20Digital%20Checklist%20of%20the%20Birds%20of%20the%20World_Version_9.pdf |website=DataZone by BirdLife |publisher=BirdLife |access-date=2025-03-29 |page=354 |date=October 2024 |version=9}}</ref> The describing authors chose the [[specific epithet]] ''cohnhafti'' to honor their colleague Mario Cohn-Haft "in recognition of his numerous and ongoing contributions to our understanding of the marvelous avifauna of Amazonian Brazil". The species' English name refers to the Brazilian state where it was discovered.<ref name=Zimmeretal/>

The Acre tody-tyrant is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC14.2/>

==Description==

The Acre tody-tyrant is about {{convert|11|cm|in|abbr=on}} long; one male weighed {{convert|9|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes apparently have the same plumage. Adults have a greenish olive head and nape with darker streaks on the crown. They have tawny [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] and a tawny wash on an otherwise greenish olive face. Their back and rump are greenish olive. Their wings are blackish with yellow outer edges and whitish inner edges on the flight feathers. Their wing [[coverts]] are black with yellowish tawny edges that show as two [[wing bar]]s. Their tail feathers are blackish with dark green inner vanes. Their throat and breast are olive-green with creamy yellowish streaks, their upper belly and flanks are unstreaked olive-green, and their lower belly and undertail coverts are sulphur-yellow. They have a cream to brownish hazel iris and gray legs and feet. Their bill is flattish with a wide base and large oval nostrils; it is black with a creamy base to the [[mandible]].<ref name="ACTT-BOW">{{cite journal |last1=del Hoyo |first1=Josep |last2=Collar |first2=Nigel |last3=Kirwan |first3=Guy M. |editor-last1=del Hoyo |editor-first1=Josep |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-last4=Christie |editor-first4=David A. |editor-last5=de Juana |editor-first5=Eduardo |display-editors=2 |title=Acre Tody-Tyrant (''Hemitriccus cohnhafti'') |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/acrtot1/1.0/introduction |url-access=limited |website=Birds of the World |publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology |access-date=2025-01-21 |location=Ithaca, NY |doi=10.2173/bow.acrtot1.01 |date=2020-03-04}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Acre tody-tyrant was initially known only from the [[Type locality (biology)|type locality]] in southeastern [[Acre (state)|Acre]] in western Brazil. The locality is very close to both northwestern Bolivia and southeastern Peru and the original authors speculated that the species also occurred in those two countries.<ref name=Zimmeretal/> It has since been confirmed in both Bolivia and Peru.<ref name="Harveyetal">{{cite journal |last1=Harvey |first1=Michael G. |last2=Lane |first2=Daniel F. |last3=Hite |first3=Justin |last4=Terrill |first4=Ryan S. |last5=Figueroa Ramírez |first5=Sheila |last6=Tilston Smith |first6=Brian |last7=Klicka |first7=John |last8=Vargas Campos |first8=Walter |display-authors=2 |editor1-last=Chakrabarty |editor1-first=Prosanta |title=Notes on bird species in bamboo in northern Madre de Dios, Peru, including the first Peruvian record of acre tody-tyrant (''Hemitriccus cohnhafti'') |journal=Occasional Papers of the Museum of Natural Science |date=2014-02-28 |volume=1 |issue=81 |pages=1–38 |doi=10.31390/opmns.081 |url=https://repository.lsu.edu/opmns/vol1/iss81/1 |publisher=Louisiana State University |location=Baton Rouge, LA |issn=2474-8935}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=van Els |first1=Paul |last2=Wijpkema |first2=Tini |last3=Wijpkema |first3=Jacob T. |title=Noteworthy records of birds from Pando including two new species for Bolivia |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |date=2023-09-07 |volume=143 |issue=3 |pages=330–345 [339–341] |doi=10.25226/bboc.v143i3.2023.a9 |doi-access=free |publisher=British Ornithologists' Club |bibcode=2023BBrOC.143..330E |issn=2513-9894}}</ref>

The Acre tody-tyrant primarily inhabits [[secondary forest]] on nutrient-poor sandy soils. These areas typically have a canopy height of under about {{convert|12|m|ft|round=5|abbr=on}} and occasionally as low as {{convert|5|m|ft|round=5|abbr=on}}. In Brazil at least they tend to have dead or dying stands of ''[[Guadua]]'' bamboo. It is not known to inhabit ''[[Varzea forest|várzea]]'' or ''[[Forest#Tropical moist|terra firme]]'' forest. In elevation it is known only below about {{convert|300|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Zimmeretal/><ref name=ACTT-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Acre tody-tyrant is believed to be a year-round resident.<ref name=ACTT-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The Acre tody-tyrant feeds on [[arthropod]]s. It feeds mostly in thick undergrowth within about {{convert|1|to|4|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} above the ground, using short upward sallies from a perch to glean prey from the underside of vegetation. It is not known to join [[mixed-species feeding flock]]s.<ref name=Zimmeretal/><ref name=ACTT-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Nothing is known about the Acre tody-tyrant's breeding biology.<ref name=ACTT-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Hemitriccus-cohnhafti |species=the Acre tody-tyrant}}
===Vocalization===

The Acre tody-tyrant's song is a trill of "tick" notes similar to songs of other ''Hemitriccus'' tody-tyrants though shorter and lower pitched. The trill usually does not have introductory notes.<ref name=Zimmeretal/><ref name=ACTT-BOW/>  It also makes "single-note ''skep'' or ''keek'' calls...or lower frequency ''kup'' calls" and sometimes combines them.<ref name=Zimmeretal/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Acre tody-tyrant as Near Threatened. Its population size is not known "but given the paucity of records and small range it is unlikely to be very large", and it is believed to be decreasing though at an unknown rate. "The species' tolerance of successional and shrubby habitats suggests that it may be able to cope with habitat degradation to some degree. There is however extensive clear-cutting ongoing in large parts of the range, particularly for the creation of cattle pastures."<ref name=IUCN/>
